profile.name: Qiongjie Huang
profile.degree: M.S. Candidate / Master of Agriculture, College of Fisheries, Huazhong Agricultural University
profile.undergraduate: Bachelor of Agriculture, College of Fisheries and Life Science, Dalian Ocean University, Sep. 2020 - Jun. 2024; GPA 4.038 / 5.000
profile.exchange: Domestic Undergraduate Exchange Program, College of Fisheries, Huazhong Agricultural University, Sep. 2022 - Jul. 2023; GPA 3.92 / 4.00
profile.target: PhD for 2027 entry preferred; fully funded/project-funded positions first, CSC-compatible supervisors as a parallel route.
profile.core_direction_cn: Clarifying the patterns and mechanisms by which human activities and climate-change-related multiple stressors affect freshwater macroinvertebrate communities.
profile.core_direction_en: Clarifying the patterns and mechanisms by which human activities and climate-change-related multiple stressors affect freshwater macroinvertebrate communities.
highlight_papers: Huang, Q., Yao, N., Bao, S., Xiong, H., Ma, B., & Wang, J. (2025). Elevational variability in the effect of pressures on the taxonomic and functional diversity of macroinvertebrate assemblages. Aquatic Sciences, 87, 68. First author. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00027-025-01194-y

Li, W., Xu, J., Huang, Q., & Wang, M. (2024). Study on the biodiversity of macroinvertebrate in the Dayang River Basin during summer based on environmental DNA and morphological analysis. Journal of Freshwater Ecology, 39(1), 2358071. Co-author. https://doi.org/10.1080/02705060.2024.2358071
Huang, Q., Sentis, A., Moi, D. A., & Wang, J. Body size shapes aquatic biotic vulnerability to co-occurring multiple stressors. Ecology Letters. First author, under review.

Wang, J., Huang, Q., Moi, D. A., Bao, S., Zhang, Y., & He, D. Anthropogenic disturbance amplifies mass ratio effects on stream macroinvertebrate biodiversity-biomass relationships. Ecological Processes. Second author, under review.

Zhang, R., Huang, Q., Yang, J., Zhou, Q., Chi, S., Zanon, F. M., Huang, M., & Wang, J. Environmental drivers shape spatial patterns of ecological uniqueness in lake macroinvertebrates across China. Water Biology and Security. Second author, under review.
research_experience: Multiple-Stressor Effects on Freshwater Macroinvertebrate Assemblages, Huazhong Agricultural University, Sep. 2024 - Jun. 2025: analyzed 195 sampling events from 134 sites in three Yangtze River tributary basins, integrating macroinvertebrate communities, 13 functional traits, land-use data, and upstream barrier records.

Used ArcGIS to extract land-use pressures; calculated taxonomic and functional diversity indices; applied PCA, Kruskal-Wallis/Dunn tests, and linear mixed models in R; found mainly additive effects and elevation-dependent shifts from antagonistic to synergistic pressure interactions.

Second-Order Meta-Analysis on Body-Size-Dependent Vulnerability, Huazhong Agricultural University / International Collaboration with France and Brazil, Sep. 2025 - present: built a dataset of 22 first-order meta-analyses covering 863 primary studies, 4,240 comparisons, and 244 effect sizes across freshwater, marine, and estuarine ecosystems.

Performed REML-based second-order meta-analysis in R/metafor with multilevel mixed-effects models and nested StudyID/EffectID random effects to test body size, ecosystem, response level, interaction type, and stressor-combination moderators.
awards: National Scholarship, Huazhong Agricultural University, Nov. 2025
First-Class Scholarship for Master's Students, Huazhong Agricultural University, Nov. 2024 and Nov. 2025
Merit Student Award (Three-Good Student), Huazhong Agricultural University, Nov. 2025
Third Prize, Liaoning Provincial College Student Smart Ocean Technology Innovation Competition, Jan. 2024
First-Class Scholarship for Undergraduate Students, Dalian Ocean University, 2021-2022
Second-Class Scholarship for Undergraduate Students, Dalian Ocean University, 2020-2021
profile.languages: Chinese native; English CET-6, proficient in academic writing and daily communication; Japanese JLPT-N2, proficient
profile.reference: Jun Wang, Associate Professor / Master's Advisor, Huazhong Agricultural University, wangjun89@mail.hzau.edu.cn

search_policy.region_rule: Prefer one verified candidate from Europe, Asia, and South America. If a region lacks a verified current opening with feasible timing, substitute the strongest longer-window full-funding prospect and explain the substitution.
must_have: Official or credible current PhD opening, recruitment call, funded position, supervisor page explicitly inviting PhD applicants, or a near-future 2027 intake page with clear timeline.
Deadline/opening window is future and feasible; preferred buffer >= 30 days, acceptable buffer 14-29 days, urgent exception < 14 days only if match and funding are unusually strong.

Supervisor has strong standing such as Professor, Associate Professor, PI, Lab Head, endowed chair, fellow, ERC/MSCA/national grant PI, academy/society fellow, centre leader, or comparable recognized title.

Research connection to freshwater macroinvertebrates, multiple anthropogenic/climate stressors, biodiversity, eDNA/morphology, functional traits/body size, river basin conservation, ecological modelling, hydrology-biodiversity links, or biomonitoring.
Evidence of recent lab activity, publications, grants, projects, students, recruitment, news, datasets, or programme activity within roughly the last three years.
Funding route is explicit or realistically actionable: project salary, studentship, tuition waiver+stipend, national scholarship, university fellowship, or CSC-host/supervisor compatibility.
For Asian candidates, exclude Japan, mainland China, Hong Kong, and Macau.
For European candidates, prioritize France, Nordic countries, and Mediterranean Europe, and require QS World University Rankings 1-400 when a QS-ranked university host exists.
For European research institutes or joint doctoral programmes, record the degree-awarding/host university QS rank where possible, or explicitly mark QS rank as needs verification.
All candidates should have a host or degree-awarding university in QS World University Rankings 1-400, unless explicitly justified as an exception.
Each candidate must include qs_rank, qs_evidence, and qs_rule_fit.
exclude: No source links or unverifiable supervisor/opening.
Deadline within 14 days and no exceptional funding/topic fit.
Only master's, visiting scholar, unpaid internship, or self-funded-only opportunity unless it is a strategic CSC host lead.
Purely marine-only or terrestrial-only work unless aquatic multiple-stressor fit is very strong.
Stale lab pages with no recent output/funding/student evidence.
Asian opportunities located in Japan, mainland China, Hong Kong, or Macau.
European university-hosted opportunities outside QS 1-400 unless there is an exceptional PI/funding reason and the exception is clearly justified.
Any university-hosted opportunity outside QS 1-400 unless there is an exceptional PI/funding/topic reason and the exception is clearly justified.
search_policy.entry_timing: Default search target is 2027 PhD entry because the candidate's M.S. is expected in Jun. 2027. Late-2026 start is acceptable only if the supervisor explicitly allows a later start or remote/preparatory timeline.
search_policy.deadline_policy: Prioritize full-funding opportunities with at least 30 days before deadline. Avoid positions closing within 14 days unless they are exceptionally strong and clearly marked as URGENT / near-deadline. Do not fill the daily quota with near-deadline items when better medium-window prospects exist.
search_policy.funding_policy: Rank project-funded PhD employment/studentships and full tuition+stipend scholarships above self-funded admission calls. CSC-compatible host routes are useful but should not replace fully funded/project-funded opportunities.
search_policy.daily_output_notes: For each pushed candidate, include deadline date or open/rolling status, preparation-window label, full-funding strength, CSC compatibility, and one concrete cold-email angle based on the CV.
search_policy.asia_exclusion_rule: Do not recommend Asian candidates in Japan, mainland China, Hong Kong, or Macau. For Asia, prioritize other locations such as Singapore, South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, Malaysia, etc., when they fit the topic and funding rules.
search_policy.europe_preference_rule: Prioritize France, Nordic countries, and Mediterranean Europe. Nordic means Denmark, Sweden, Norway, Finland, Iceland. Mediterranean Europe means Spain, Portugal, Italy, Greece, Malta, Cyprus, Croatia, Slovenia, and comparable Mediterranean-coast institutions. European candidates must still satisfy the global QS 1-400 rule unless explicitly justified as an exception.
search_policy.global_qs_rule: For all candidates in Europe, Asia, South America, or substitute regions, the host or degree-awarding university should be QS World University Rankings 1-400. Record qs_rank, qs_evidence, and qs_rule_fit. For research institutes, national institutes, labs, or joint doctoral programmes without one single QS-ranked institution, identify the degree-awarding university, enrolling university, or formal host university and apply the QS 1-400 rule to that university. If QS cannot be verified, mark QS as needs verification and do not treat the candidate as top-priority unless funding/topic fit is exceptional.

daily_briefs.summary: Registered the first sent outreach email to Jeremy J. Piggott, added manual email supervision rules for HQJ123@webmail.hzau.edu.cn, and added Frederik De Laender from CV_FDL.pdf as a ranked exception target.
daily_briefs.search_notes: Piggott is now marked first_contact_sent with inbox check on 2026-07-22 and earliest follow-up on 2026-07-29. De Laender is added with QS #801, Google Scholar h-index 40, very strong project/supervision scale, and priority score 49, placing him above Garcia Giron but below QS/H-index stronger targets.

daily_briefs.summary: Reset active supervisor list to 8 teacher-nominated European targets and re-ranked them mainly by QS/host rank, H-index, and team/project scale.
daily_briefs.search_notes: Ranking formula changed per user instruction: QS/host rank 40%, H-index 35%, team/project scale 25%. New order: Soininen, Zarfl, Piggott, Alahuhta, Brosse, Sentis, Graco-Roza, Garcia-Giron. This intentionally lowers Sentis despite strong research fit because Aix-Marseille QS and personal H-index are weaker than the top-ranked targets.

candidates.csc_fit: CSC plausible as fallback; try active salaried RESDOC route first.
candidates.requirements: Prepare RESDOC-style research plan, CV, publication list, transcripts and English evidence; obtain Soininen support before submission.
candidates.research_directions: Aquatic community ecology, biogeography, macroecology, benthic diatoms, phytoplankton, bacteria, freshwater conservation and biomonitoring.
candidates.match_relation: Very strong for macroecology, freshwater biodiversity and modelling; less macroinvertebrate-specific than Sentis/Piggott but has a live funding window.
candidates.hats: Professor; founder of Aquatic Community Ecology Group; chair of Biogeosciences research programme; extensive PhD supervision.

candidates.email_subject: Prospective RESDOC PhD applicant: freshwater beta diversity and multiple stressors
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Professor Soininen,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University working on freshwater macroinvertebrate responses to human activities and climate-change-related multiple stressors. I am particularly interested in your Aquatic Community Ecology Group because of its work on aquatic biogeography, community assembly, biodiversity patterns and biomonitoring across freshwater systems.

My first-author Aquatic Sciences paper analysed taxonomic and functional diversity of macroinvertebrates from 195 sampling events across three Yangtze tributary basins, linking land-use pressure, upstream barriers, functional traits and mixed models. I am also leading an Ecology Letters manuscript on body-size-dependent vulnerability to co-occurring stressors.

For a 2027 PhD, I hope to develop a project on trait-based freshwater community change across environmental gradients, potentially connecting macroinvertebrate functional diversity with broader aquatic community ecology and RESDOC themes on biodiversity resilience. May I send you my CV and a one-page research idea to ask whether this could fit your group?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang
candidates.notes: Teacher-nominated and already known from old system; kept top three because of active Helsinki RESDOC funding. Priority score reweighted on 2026-07-14: QS/host rank 40%, H-index 35%, team/project scale 25%. Research fit remains a threshold and email-angle factor, not the main ranking driver. Email draft rewritten on 2026-07-14 to use a supervisor-specific angle rather than a generic template.
candidates.application_deadline: 2026-08-31 for current University of Helsinki RESDOC salaried doctoral researcher call.
candidates.qs_evidence: QS TopUniversities profile states University of Helsinki is ranked #=123 in QS World University Rankings 2027.
candidates.qs_rule_fit: Yes - QS #=123 is within the old 1-400 range.
candidates.h_index_evidence: Google Scholar profile snippet reports h-index 71 for Janne Soininen.
candidates.team_project_scale: Large professor-led University of Helsinki aquatic community ecology group plus active RESDOC salaried doctoral researcher call.
candidates.ranking_basis: Priority score reweighted on 2026-07-14: QS/host rank 40%, H-index 35%, team/project scale 25%. Research fit remains a threshold and email-angle factor, not the main ranking driver.
candidates.reply_strategy: Before sending, use the ChatGPT prompt pack to polish the targeted draft. After sending, record first_contact_date and switch status to first_contact_sent.

candidates.email_subject: Prospective PhD applicant: river fragmentation, barriers and freshwater trait responses
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Professor Zarfl,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University studying how human activities and climate-change-related multiple stressors shape freshwater macroinvertebrate communities. Your work on river fragmentation, hydropower, dams and environmental systems analysis is especially relevant to a part of my current dataset that integrates upstream barrier records with community and trait responses.

In my first-author Aquatic Sciences paper, I analysed 195 sampling events from three Yangtze tributary basins, combining land-use pressure, barriers, taxonomic diversity, functional diversity and mixed models. My current Ecology Letters manuscript examines body-size-dependent vulnerability to co-occurring stressors across aquatic systems.

For a 2027 PhD, I would like to explore how river infrastructure and land-use pressure jointly alter freshwater community structure and trait composition at basin scale. May I send you my CV and a short proposal outline to ask whether this direction could fit your Environmental Systems Analysis group?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang
candidates.notes: Teacher-nominated. Germany is outside old Europe preference set, but QS and river-barrier fit are strong. Priority score reweighted on 2026-07-14: QS/host rank 40%, H-index 35%, team/project scale 25%. Research fit remains a threshold and email-angle factor, not the main ranking driver. Email draft rewritten on 2026-07-14 to use a supervisor-specific angle rather than a generic template.

candidates.europe_priority_fit: No - Germany is strong but not France/Nordic/Mediterranean under the earlier preference rule.

candidates.email_subject: Prospective PhD applicant: experimental and trait-based freshwater multiple-stressor ecology
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Professor Piggott,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University working on freshwater macroinvertebrate responses to multiple anthropogenic and climate-related stressors. I am very interested in the Piggott Lab because your group directly addresses how climate change and other stressors interact to affect freshwater biodiversity and ecosystem functioning.

My first-author Aquatic Sciences paper analysed macroinvertebrate taxonomic and functional diversity across 195 sampling events in three Yangtze tributary basins, integrating land-use pressure, upstream barriers, functional traits and linear mixed models. I am also leading an Ecology Letters manuscript on how body size shapes aquatic biotic vulnerability to co-occurring stressors.

For PhD study, I hope to combine field-derived trait patterns with more mechanistic or experimental multiple-stressor questions, especially around climate, nutrients, sediment or land-use pressure. May I send you my CV and a brief research idea to ask whether there may be a fit with your lab for 2027 entry?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang
candidates.notes: User confirmed piggott is Jeremy J. Piggott. Ireland is not in old France/Nordic/Mediterranean preference, but topic and QS fit are outstanding. Priority score reweighted on 2026-07-14: QS/host rank 40%, H-index 35%, team/project scale 25%. Research fit remains a threshold and email-angle factor, not the main ranking driver. Email draft rewritten on 2026-07-14 to use a supervisor-specific angle rather than a generic template.

candidates.qs_rule_fit: Yes - QS #75 is within the old 1-400 range.
candidates.europe_priority_fit: No - Ireland is strong but not France/Nordic/Mediterranean under the earlier preference rule.

candidates.team_project_scale: Dedicated Piggott Lab at Trinity with freshwater multiple-stressor research and prior Trinity doctoral award route.
candidates.ranking_basis: Priority score reweighted on 2026-07-14: QS/host rank 40%, H-index 35%, team/project scale 25%. Research fit remains a threshold and email-angle factor, not the main ranking driver.

candidates.email_subject: Prospective PhD applicant: freshwater macroecology, traits and community change
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Dr. Alahuhta,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University working on trait-based responses of freshwater macroinvertebrate communities to human activities and climate-change-related stressors. Your research on freshwater organism biogeography, macroecology, aquatic plant diversity and geodiversity-biodiversity relationships is highly relevant to the spatial and trait-based questions I hope to develop further.

In my first-author Aquatic Sciences paper, I analysed 195 macroinvertebrate sampling events from three Yangtze tributary basins, combining land-use pressure, upstream barriers, taxonomic diversity, functional diversity and mixed models. I am also leading an Ecology Letters manuscript on body-size-dependent vulnerability to co-occurring stressors.

Although my current organism group is macroinvertebrates, I see strong methodological connections with your freshwater macroecological work, especially functional diversity, spatial turnover and environmental gradients. May I send my CV and a short research-fit note to ask whether a 2027 PhD route at Oulu could be possible?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang

candidates.email_subject: Prospective PhD applicant: freshwater biodiversity, traits and anthropogenic pressure
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Professor Brosse,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University studying freshwater biodiversity responses to human activities and climate-change-related multiple stressors. I am drawn to your work on global freshwater fish biodiversity, functional diversity and anthropogenic impacts because it addresses the same broad question I hope to pursue: how human pressure reshapes freshwater communities across scales.

My first-author Aquatic Sciences paper analysed macroinvertebrate taxonomic and functional diversity across 195 sampling events in three Yangtze tributary basins, integrating land-use pressure, upstream barriers, functional traits and mixed models. I am also leading an Ecology Letters manuscript on body-size-dependent vulnerability to co-occurring stressors.

For a PhD, I would like to extend my macroinvertebrate trait and stressor background toward broader freshwater biodiversity synthesis, potentially comparing responses across taxa, traits and river basins. May I send you my CV and a short research idea for your advice on whether this could fit your group?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang

candidates.email_subject: Prospective PhD applicant: body size, warming and freshwater multiple stressors
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Dr. Sentis,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University working on freshwater macroinvertebrate responses to human activities and climate-change-related multiple stressors. I am especially interested in your ClimateCountDown project because it links thermal vulnerability, food-web structure and body size in freshwater communities.

My first-author Aquatic Sciences paper analysed taxonomic and functional diversity of macroinvertebrates across 195 sampling events from three Yangtze tributary basins, integrating land-use pressure, upstream barriers, functional traits and mixed models. I am also leading the Ecology Letters manuscript with you and collaborators on body-size-dependent vulnerability to co-occurring stressors.

For a 2027 PhD, I hope to build on this background by connecting macroinvertebrate trait data, warming-related vulnerability and multiple-stressor synthesis in freshwater systems. May I send you my CV and a short research-fit note to ask whether there may be a suitable route within ClimateCountDown or a related project?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang

candidates.email_subject: Prospective PhD applicant: functional turnover and freshwater stressor gradients
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Dr. Graco-Roza,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University working on freshwater macroinvertebrate communities under multiple anthropogenic and climate-related stressors. I am interested in your Ecological Synthesis work because it focuses on biodiversity change, species turnover, functional trait replacement and human pressures across large datasets.

My first-author Aquatic Sciences paper analysed 195 macroinvertebrate sampling events from three Yangtze tributary basins, combining land-use pressure, upstream barriers, functional traits and mixed models. I am also leading an Ecology Letters manuscript on body-size-dependent vulnerability to co-occurring stressors.

For PhD study, I would like to examine whether freshwater communities show predictable taxonomic and functional turnover along multiple-stressor gradients, using macroinvertebrates as a trait-rich study system. May I send you my CV and a short project idea to ask whether this could fit a 2027 Helsinki route, including RESDOC if appropriate?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang

candidates.email_subject: Prospective PhD applicant: freshwater multiple stressors, community stability and trait responses
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Professor De Laender,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University studying how human activities and climate-change-related multiple stressors shape freshwater macroinvertebrate communities. I am very interested in your work on environmental change, biodiversity-ecosystem functioning, community stability and theoretical ecology, especially your use of modelling, experiments and meta-analysis to understand community responses to stressors.

My first-author Aquatic Sciences paper analysed taxonomic and functional diversity of macroinvertebrates across 195 sampling events in three Yangtze tributary basins, integrating land-use pressure, upstream barriers, functional traits and mixed models. I am also leading an Ecology Letters manuscript on body-size-dependent vulnerability to co-occurring multiple stressors.

For a 2027 PhD, I hope to develop a project that connects empirical trait-based freshwater evidence with theory-driven questions on coexistence, stability and ecosystem functioning under multiple environmental changes. May I send you my CV and a short research idea to ask whether this direction could fit your lab or a possible funding route?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang
candidates.notes: Added from user-provided CV_FDL.pdf on 2026-07-15. Strong H-index/project-scale and excellent conceptual fit, but University of Namur QS #801 makes this a ranking-rule exception and lowers system priority.
candidates.application_deadline: No current 2027 project-specific deadline verified; supervisor-first inquiry and funding-route check needed.
candidates.deadline_priority_notes: Lower urgency than QS-strong targets. Send after the first tier unless teacher specifically prioritises De Laender. If contacted, foreground modelling/meta-analysis/stability rather than macroinvertebrate taxonomy alone.
candidates.qs_evidence: Study.eu University of Namur page lists QS World University Rankings 2027 rank #801; official QS page should be rechecked if ranking precision becomes important.
candidates.qs_rule_fit: No under the earlier QS 1-400 rule; retained as a user-provided CV exception because H-index, project scale and conceptual fit are strong.
candidates.europe_priority_fit: No - Belgium is strong geographically accessible Europe but not France/Nordic/Mediterranean under the earlier preference rule.
candidates.supervision_notes: Do not send immediately unless teacher wants him next. Good backup target for theory/community ecology and multiple-stressor synthesis.
candidates.reply_strategy: If positive: send CV and a 1-page idea linking Huang's Ecology Letters body-size synthesis to community stability/coexistence under environmental change. If funding-uncertain: ask about CSC-compatible PhD hosting, FNRS/FRIA routes and UNamur doctoral registration.

candidates.email_subject: Prospective PhD applicant: freshwater functional diversity and macroecological gradients
candidates.email_draft:
Dear Dr. García Girón,

I am Qiongjie Huang, an M.S. candidate at Huazhong Agricultural University working on freshwater macroinvertebrate responses to human activities and climate-change-related multiple stressors. I am interested in your research on freshwater macrophyte macroecology, functional and phylogenetic beta diversity, and large-scale biodiversity gradients.

My first-author Aquatic Sciences paper analysed taxonomic and functional diversity of macroinvertebrate assemblages across 195 sampling events from three Yangtze tributary basins, integrating land-use pressure, upstream barriers, functional traits and mixed models. I am also leading an Ecology Letters manuscript on body-size-dependent vulnerability to co-occurring stressors.

Although my current taxonomic focus differs from macrophytes, I see a clear methodological connection through functional diversity, spatial turnover and freshwater biodiversity change under human pressure. May I send you my CV and a short research idea to ask whether there may be a suitable doctoral route at Universidad de León?

Best regards,
Qiongjie Huang

outreach_supervision.monitoring_note: The system tracks sent dates, response status, next action dates and strategy. It does not automatically log in to the school mailbox; paste replies or update status manually after checking HQJ123@webmail.hzau.edu.cn.
rules: Do not send a follow-up within the first 7 days unless the professor explicitly asks for materials.
First real follow-up should normally wait 10-14 days after the first email.
If positive or curious reply: answer within 24 hours with CV, 1-page fit note, and a concise funding/timeline question.
If neutral or funding-uncertain reply: ask whether external funding/CSC or departmental doctoral routes are feasible.
If no response after first follow-up: wait another 14 days before deciding whether to close or send a final very short note.
If rejection: thank briefly and ask only if they can suggest a better-fitting colleague.
